items.q: How do you say 'I will drink coffee tomorrow' in German?
items.why: Students translate 'will' as 'will'. The German 'will' (from wollen) means 'want', not future. Future = WERDEN + infinitive. Also, German often uses present + time word ('Ich trinke morgen Kaffee') for near future.
Spelled identically to English 'bald' but means SOON. 'Bis bald!' = 'See you soon!'. The English 'bald' translates as 'kahl' or 'glatzköpfig'. Pure faux ami.

items.q: What is the difference between 'ich will' and 'ich möchte'?
items.correct: Ich will = I want (strong, direct); ich möchte = I would like (polite).
items.why: Students learn wollen as 'to want' and use it casually. 'Ich will...' sounds DEMANDING/childish. Polite German uses ICH MÖCHTE (Konjunktiv form of mögen) for 'I would like'. Essential for job/interview contexts.
Correct answer: Privatise eastern firms. “Run elections” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Nostalgia for the DDR. “Eastern hostility” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was the Solidaritatszuschlag introduced?
items.why: The real figure is 1991. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
